Boyle's  narrative  is  probably  fictitious,  but  that  of  Castlernan  bears  marks  of  authen- 
ticity. The  latter's  visit  to  Philadelphia  took  place  in  1710,  Boyle's  vovages  have 
been  often  reprinted;  but  Castleman's  relation  is  onlv  to  be  found  in  the  early  editions. 
This  work  has  been  attributed  to  Benj.  Victor;   also  to  Daniel  Defoe.

CHILI.  37 

Mcusel  attributes  this  anonymous  work  to  the  Abbe  Vidaurre,  but  Molina  speaks  of 
it  as  if  by  a  different  author.  The  American  translator  of  Molina  has  made  large 
extracts  from  it,  from  which  it  appears  to  be,  though  concise,  a  work  of  considerable 
authority.  A  map  of  the  country,  a  plan  of  Santiago,  the  capital,  and  other  places, 
all  made  from  accurate  observations,  add  to  the  value  of  this  work.  Translated  into

Thomas,  in  his  **  History  of  Printing  in  America,"  Vol.  i.  372,  has  a  long  account  of 
the  arrest  of  Daniel  Fowle  on  suspicion  of  being  the  printer  of  this  obnoxious  pamph- 
let. Fowle  himself  printed  an  account  of  the  indignities  he  suffered  in  this  behalf, 
entitled,  "Total  Eclipse  of  Liberty."  Of  the  work  itself,  "Tom  Thumb,"  which 
occasioned  all  this  mischief,  not  more  than  three  or  four  copies  are  known,  the  general 
court  of  Massachusetts  Bay  having  ordered  it  "  to  be  burnt  by  the  hands  of  the  common 
hangman,  in  King  Street,  Boston."

The  picture  of  King  Philip,  first  published  in  this  edition,  is  fictitious;  so  also  is  that 
of  Col.  Church  {see  Rich,  Vol.  i.  198),  who  expresses  the  opinion  that  they  "  are 
probably  among  the  first  specimens  of  engraving  done  in  New  England."  The  latter 
is  said  to  have  been  taken  from  a  picture  of  Charles  Churchill,  the  poet,  with  the  addi- 
tion of  a  powder-horn  slung  around  the  neck.      This  edition  is  rare. 

Church.  The  History  of  King  Philip's  War,  Commonly 
Called  The  Great  Indian  War,  of  1675  and  1676.  Also  of  the 
French  and  Indian  Wars  at  the  Eastward,  in  1689,  1690,  1692, 
1696,  and  1704.  By  Thomas  Church,  Esq.  With  Numerous 
Notes  to  Explain  the  Situation  of  the  Places  of  Battles,  the  Par- 
ticular Geography  of  the  Ravaged  Country,  and  the  Lives  of  the 
Principal  Persons  Engaged  in  those  Wars.  Also,  An  Appendix, 
Containing  an  Account  of  the  treatment  of  the  natives  by  the 
early  voyagers,  the  settlement  of  N.  England  by  the  forefathers, 
the  Pequot  War,  narrative  of  persons  carried  into  captivity, 
anecdotes  of  the  Indians,  and  the  most  important  Indian  wars  to 
the  time  of  the  Creek  War.  By  Samuel  G.  Drake.  Boston.

58  CHURCHILL. 

Usually  called  Churchill's  collection,  as  he  was  the  publisher.  Two  additional  volumes, 
making  the  hfth  and  sixth,  were  printed  in  1733.  All  these  volumes  were  reprinted  or 
reissued  with  the  dates  of  1744-1747  and  1752.  Two  more  volumes  were  printed  in 
1745  by  Thomas  Osborne,  from  materials  in  the  library  of  the  Earl  of  Oxford,  whence 
they  are  generally  known  as  the  "  Oxford  Collection  of  Voyages.  London  :  Thomas  Os- 
borne. MDCcxLvii."  2  1.,  pp.  xii,  Ixiii,  873,  15  1.  Vol.  viii.  Title,  pp.  932.  h.  These 
eight  volumes  are  usually  found  together.  The  following  are  the  narratives  contained 
in  the  six  volumes,  which  relate  to  America,  in  Churchill's  collection  :  Vol.  i.  Brawern 
and  Herckemann's  Voyage  to  Chili  in  1642  and  1643,  '^''°"^  ^^^  Dutch  edition  of 
1649;  Capt.  John  Monck's  Voyage  in  1619  and  1620,  to  Hudson's  Straits,  to  discover 
a  passage  between  Greenland  and  America,  to  the  West  Indies,  For  the  original  edi- 
tion of  this  work  in  the  Swedish  language,  see  Munk  (J.)  — Vol.  11.  Nieuhotf' s  remark- 
able Voyages  and  Travels  into  Brazil,  and  the  best  parts  of  the  East  Indies.  Translated 
from  the  Dutch  ;  Capt.  John  Smith's  True  Travels  and  Adventures  into  Europe,  Asia, 
Africa,  and  America,  from  the  year  1592  to  1629}  Two  Journals.  The  first  kept  by 
seven  sailors  in  the  Island  of  St.  Maurice  in  Greenland  in  1633  and  1634,  who  pass'd 
the  winter  and  died  there.  The  Second,  kept  by  seven  other  sailors  who  wintered  at 
Spitzbergen  in  1633  and  1634;  An  Account  of  Iceland  sent  to  Mons.  de  la  Mothe  de 
Vaycr  by  La  Peyrere.  From  the  French  ;  An  Account  of  Greenland,  from  M.  La 
Peyrere.  From  the  French ;  James's  Strange  and  Dangerous  Voyage  for  the  Discovery 
of  a  North-West  Passage  in  1631  and  1632.  First  printed  in  1633;  The  Life  of 
Columbus,  by  his  Son,  and  of  his  Discovery  of  the  West  Indies,  by  his  Son,  D.  Fer- 
dinand Columbus.  —  Vol.  in.  Ovalle's  Historical  Relation  of  the  Kingdom  of  Chili. 
Abriged  from  the  Spanish.  First  printed  at  Rome,  in  1646;  Sir  William  Monson's 
Naval  Tracts,  in  six  books.  Including  the  discoveries  of  the  Spaniards  and  Portuguese. 
Now  first  published.  —  Vol.  iv.  Gemelli  Carreri's  Voyage  round  the  World.  Trans- 
lated from  the  Italian.  First  printed  at  Naples,  1699;  Voyage  of  Anthony  Sepp  and 
Ant.  Behme  from  Spain  to  Paraguaria.  Translated  from  the  Dutch.  First  printed 
at  Nuremburg  in  1697J  Techo's  History  of  the  Provinces  of  Paraguay,  Rio  de  la 
Plata,  Parana,  Chili,  Guaira,  and  Urvaica.  Translated  from  the  Latin ;  Pelham's 
Account  of  the  Preservation  of  Eight  men  in  Greenland,  left  there  in  1630.  —  VoL  v. 
Barbot's  Description  of  North  and  South  Guinea  and  Angola,  with  a  new  relation  of 
Guiana,  and  of  the  rivers  of  Amazon  and  Oronoco ,  General  Observations,  and  an 
account  of  the  discovery  of  America  from  Herrera's  History  of  the  West  Indies.  With 
a  brief  description  of  the  Caribbee  Islands  of  America.  —  Vol.  vi.  Colonel  Norwood's 
Voyage  to  Virginia,  made  in  16095  Captain  Thomas  Philips's  Voyage  to  Mountseradoe 
in  Africa,  and  to  Barbadoes  and  the  Island  of  St.  Thomas,  in  1693  and  1694;  John 
Gatonbe's  Voyage  into  the  North- West  Passage  undertaken  in  16125  The  Mosquito 
Indian  and  his  Golden  River,  being  a  familiar  description  of  the  Mosquito  Kingdom  in 
America.  With  a  relation  ot  their  strange  customs,  etc.  Written  in  16995  An  Ac- 
count of  the  Wonderful  Preservation  of  the  Ship  Terra  Nova,  of  London,  homeward 
bound  frorr  Virginia.      By  C.  May.

To  this  edition  a  copious  index  is  added,  and  there  is  some  little  difference  in  the 
arrangement  of  the  articles.  The  account  of  books  of  voyages  ami  travels  is  also 
brought  down  to  the  present  year.  Many  of  the  works  in  this  collection  have  never 
appeared  elsewhere  in  English.  Sir  James  Macintosh  remarks  :  "  The  old  voyages 
are  always  more  picturesque  and  poetical  than  modern.  Churchill  and  Harris  furnish 
a  great  abundance  of  Indian  imagery."  And  Richarderie  observes  :  "This  collection 
is  very  valuable  j  its  place  cannot  be  supplied  by  recurring  to  the  original  works,  as  a 
great  part  of  them  are  first  published  in  it  from  the  manuscript."  In  the  so-callrd 
Mondidier  Catalogue,  a  copy  is  described  as  being  on  the  largest  paper.  It  is  a  work 
which  sells  at  a  price  below  its  real  value.  In  1825,  Wheatley's  copy  sold  for  £12 
12s.  5  1840,  Jas.  Bohn,  mor.,£i8  iSs.j  Merly,  £125  Reed,  £13  6s.  5  Splidt,  £15  5s.  j 
Dr.  Heath,  £15  ;  Maddison,  £205  and  Joshua  Smith,  £22  iis.  6d.  It  now  ranges 
at  from  £4  to  £6  6s.  in  booksellers'  catalogues.

This  work  is  a  translation,  with  some  augmentations,  of  the  introduction  to 
Churchill's  collection  of  voyages.  The  French  editor,  in  his  preface,  apologises  for 
the  cruelties  of  the  Spaniards  to  the  Indians  in  this  summary  way:  "It  was  difficult 
to  use  moderation  towards  beings  human  only  in  figure 5  finally,  of  these  savages  were 
made  men,  of  these  animals  were  made  Christians.  God  is  adored  where  he  was 
not  known.  The  mission  truly  has  not  been  like  that  of  the  first  apostles,  but  the 
Gospel  has  been  preached  and  received."

Probably  the  finest  production  of  Franklin's  press,  and  really  a  splendid  specimen  of 
the  art.  The  title-page  is  rubricated.  The  translator  was  Judge  James  Logan  5  and 
Franklin,  in  his  address,  "The  Printer  to  the  Reader,"  calls  it,  "this  first  translation 
of  a  classic  in  this  Western  World,"  which  is  not  strictly  correct.  Sandy's  translation 
of  Ovid  was  made  in  Virginia  about  ninety  years  earlier,  but  printed  and  published 
in  London  j  and  Franklin  himself  had  printed  Cato's  "Moral  Distiches,"  Englished 
in  couplets,  in  1739,  which  translation  is  attributed  to  James  Logan.  See  our  No. 
1 1548.  See  also  Epictetus,  infra.  It  is,  doubtless,  the  second  classic  author  trans- 
lated and  printed  \n  North  America,  and  is  eagerly  sought  after  by  American  collectors. 
Uncut  copies  are  among  the  rarest  of  rare  books.  I  know  of  but  four  copies — the  two 
mentioned  above,  one  belonging  to  Mr.  William  Menzies,  and  one  to  Mr.  C.  E.  Mann, 
the  latter  being  the  copy  described  in  Stevens'  Nuggets,  No.  551,  is  on  "large  and 
thick    paper,  and  unique"  —  a  mistake.      It  was  reprinted  in  England  as 

Cicero.  Cato  Major:  or,  a  Treatise  on  Old  Age,  by  M. 
Tullius  Cicero,  with  Explanatory  Notes  from  the  Roman  His- 
tory.     By  the  Honourable  James  Logan,  Esq.,  President  of  the 


CIE9A    DE    LEON,  73 

Coancil,  and  Chief  Justice  of  the  Province  of  Philadelphia. 
Fourth  Edition.  Philadelphia^  Printed ;  Glasgow^  Re-printed  By 
R.   Urie.    M.DCC.LViii.     i2mo,  pp.  168,  2  1.  13041 

Cicero.  M.  T.  Cicero's  Cato  Major,  or  Discourse  on  Old 
Age.  Addressed  to  Titus  Pomponius  Atticus.  With  Explan- 
atory Notes.  By  Benj.  Franklin  ll.d.  Philadelphia:  Printed 
by  B.  Franklin.  London :  Re-printed  for  Fielding  and  Walker. 
MDCCLXxviii.     8vo,  2  1.,  pp.  163.     Portrait.  13042 

In  this  edition,  Dr.  Franklin  is  made  to  appear  as  the  editor;  doubtless  a  publisher's 
trick,  to  increase  the  sale  of  the  book.  See  M.  R.,  lix.  467.  In  the  last  edition  the 
title  is  : 

Cicero.  Cato  Major;  or,  a  Discourse  on  Old  Age,  ad- 
dressed to  Titus  Pomponius  Atticus,  with  Explanatory  Notes, 
by  Benjamin  Franklin,  ll.d.      Philadelphia:    W.  Duane.      Svo, 

pp.  251-357.  13043 

This  was  intended  to  form  part  of  Duane's  edition  of  Franklin's  works,  but  was 
omitted. 

Cicero,  pseudonym.     See  [Galloway  (Joseph)]. 

C1E9A  DE  Leon  (P.  de).  Parte  Primera  |  Dela  chronica  del 
Peru.  Que  tracta  la  demarca- |cion  de  sus  prouincias:  la  de- 
scripcion  dellas.  Las  |  fundaciones  de  las  nueuas  ciudades.  Los 
ritos  y|costumbres  de  los  indios.  Y  otras  cosas  estrafias  |  dignas 
de  ser  sabidas.  Fecha  por  Pedro  d  Cie^ajde  Leon  vezino  de 
Seuilla.  I  1553.  |Con  priuillegio  Real.  |  [Colophon:]  Impressa  en 
Seuilla  en  casa  de  Martin  \  de  montesdoca.  Acahose  a  quini.e  de  \  Mar^o 
de  mill  y  quinientos  y\cinquenta  y  tres  anos.  Folio,  10  1.,  Text, 
cxxxiiij  1.  j.c.B.  13044 

"The  first  part  only  was  printed}  the  second  and  third  part  were  seen  in  MS.  in 
Madrid  some  time  ago,  but  it  is  not  known  what  became  of  them."  —  Rich.  The 
manuscript  of  the  third  part  is  now  in  Mr.  Lenox's  collection.  Mr.  Prescott  bestows 
much  praise  on  the  work,  and  Mr.  C.  R.  Markham  characterizes  it  as  "one  of  the 
most  remarkable  literary  productions  of  the  age  of  Spanish  Conquest  in  America."  It 
is,  in  fact,  the  only  book  which  exhibits  the  "physical  aspect  of  the  country  as  it 
existed  under  the  elaborate  culture  of  the  Incas."  —  Prescott,  ii.  328.  As  long  ago 
as  1835,  Mr.  Rich  priced  this  at  £5  5s.;  it  would  probably  sell  for  five  times  the  price  now 
(1870).  In  a  catalogue  of  Puttick  and  Sampson,  for  i860,  an  edition  is  described  as 
"An vers  en  Casa  de  Juan  Steelsio,  1553,"  which  is  probably  the  same  as  described 
in  our  note  to  No.  13046.

An  entirely  different  edition,  though  of  the  same  year  and  place,  apparently  pirated

1554,"  collation  the  same  as  Bellero's  edition,  but  the  map,  if  found  in  it,  is  Bellero's.

CLAGGETT. 


91 


This  interesting  volume  is  a  fine  specimen  of  Bradford's  printing;  and  the  impres- 
sions having  been  chierty  distributed  among  the  Indians,  and  bv  them  destroyed,  it  has 
become  a  book  of  almost  matchless  rarity.  It  is  the  earliest  Book  of  Common 
Prayer  in  any  tongue  in  this  country.  Mr.  Claesse  is  said  to  be  the  translator,  but  the 
greater  portion  of  it  was  prepared,  some  years  previously,  by  a  Mr.  Freeman,  a  Calvinist 
minister  at  Schenectady,  N.  Y.  In  the  "  Hist.  Mag.,"  1.  14,  it  is  said  to  be  without 
date  or  imprint,  which  is  an  error,  the  copy  described  being  imperfect.  Some  inter- 
esting particulars  concerning  this  work  will  be  found  in  Humphrey's  **  Historical 
Account  of  the  Soc.  for  Prop,  the  Gospel,"  c.  xi.,  Hawkins'  "Notes,"  O'Calla- 
ghan's  "  List  of  Bibles,"  etc.  Lowndes  quotes  it  at  two  guineas;  it  would  now  com- 
mand ten  times  the  amount.  Mr.  T.  Bailey  Myers,  of  New  York,  possesses  an 
uncut  copy.      Mr.  James  Lenox  and  Mr.  H,  C.  Murphy  have  fine  copies.

Bartlett's  "  Bibliog.  of  R.  I."  quotes  from  Prof.  Elton's  note  to  (Callender's  "Hist. 
Disc,"  p.  210),  the  title  of  another  tract  by  Clark,  said  to  have  been  issued  the  pre- 
ceding year,  called  "A  Brief  Discourse  touching  New  England,  as  to  the  matter  in 
hand,  and  to  that  part  of  it,  sci.  Rhode  Island,  where  my  residence  is,  together  with 
the  occasion  of  my  going  out  with  others  from  the  Mathatusets  Bay,  and  tlie  many 
providential  occurrences  that  directed  us  thereto,  and  pitched  us  thereon."  This 
abstract  fills  three  pages,  and  is  followed  by  "  A  faithful  and  true  relation  of  the  per- 
secution of  Obadiah  Holmes,  John  Crandall  and  John  Clark,  merely  for  conscience 
sake  towards  God,  by  the  principal  members  of  the  church  or  commonwealth  of  the 
Mathatusets  in  New  England,  which  rules  over  that  part  of  the  world  j  wherein  is 
shown  their  discourteous  entertainment  of  strangers,  and  how  that  spirit  by  which 
they  acted,  would  order  the  whole  world  if  either  brought  under  them,  or  should  come 
in  unto  them.  Drawn  forth  by  the  aforesaid  John  Clark,  not  so  much  to  answer  the 
Importunity  of  Friends,  as  to  stop  the  mouths,  and  slanderous  reports  of  such  as  are 
Enemies  to  the  Cross  of  Christ,"  etc.  Dr.  John  Clark  was  the  founder  of  the  first 
Baptist  church  in  Newport,  in  1644.

Comes  down  to  the  end  of  the  fifteenth  century,  and  is  all  that  was  published  of  the 
work.  The  "Edinburgh  Review"  is  rather  severe  in  its  criticisms,  but  allows  the 
author  credit  for  his  bibliographical  researches.  The  appendix  contains,  among  other 
things,  Galvano's  Discoveries  of  the  World,  and  Locke's  History  of  Navigation,  and 
Explanatory  Catalogue  of  Voyages  from  Churchill's  collection.     See  M.  R.,  xlv.  403.

"The  author  considers  that  the  prior  discovery  by  Sebastian  Cabot,  in  1497,  of  the 
coast  of  a  large  portion  of  the  continent  of  North  America,  and  the  subsequent 
grants  by  English  sovereigns,  of  Nova  Scotia,  Connecticut,  Massachusetts,  and  Vir- 
ginia, with  their  western  limits  of  the  latter  three  extendmg  to  the  South  Sea,  gave 
the  English  colonists  a  full  right  to  the  vast  region  embraced  within  these  limits,  and 
that  the  French,  in  all  their  occupations  south  of  the  St.  Lawrence  and  the  great  lakes, 
were  encroachments.  —  Monthly  Re'vieiv^  xiii.  400.  For  some  letters  of  Clarke,  ite 
"Mass.  Hist.  Soc.  Coll.,"  v.  4.  The  papers  added  are  by  Benjamin  Franklin.  See 
Franklin's  Works,  11.  311,  and  Bancroft's  "  Hist,  of  the  U.  S.,"  iv.  213.

"Clavigero  was  a  native  of  Vera  Cruz  (born  173 1,  died  at  Bologna,  1787),  a  Jesuit 
and  a  thorough  antiquarian,  who  spent  thirty  years  of  active  research  into  the  archae- 
ology and  antiquities  of  Mexico.  His  book,  originally  published  in  Italian,  is  a  mine 
of  precious  historical  documents,  and  contains  valuable  lists  of  others  in  the  Mendoza, 


CLAVIGERO. 


137 


the  Vatican,  and  the  Boturini  collections.  All  the  other  books  that  have  been  elaborated 
since  on  the  same  subject,  instead  of  superseding  Clavigero's,  have  tended  rather  to 
magnify  its   importance."  —  Stevens.      See  also   Prescott's   Mexico,  i.  52,  and   M.  R.,

There  are  two  editions  of  this  "  Memoir,"  in  the  first,  which  is  very  rare,  the 
author  expresses  an  opinion  that  the  Spaniards  had,  at  one  time,  a  colony  in  the 
country  of  the  Onondagas.  In  the  second  issue  this  opinion  is  omitted.  Later  re- 
searches seem  to  indicate  that  his  first  opinion  was  correct.

[Cluny  (Alexander).]  The  American  Traveller;  or,  Obser- 
vations on  the  Present  State,  Culture  and  Commerce  of  the 
British  Colonies  in  America,  And  the  further  Improvements  of 
which  they  are  capable;  u^ith  An  Account  of  the  Exports,  Im- 
ports and  Returns  of  each  Colony  respectively,  —  and  of  the 
Numbers  of  British  Ships  and  Seamen,  Merchants,  Traders  and 
Manufacturers  employed  by  all  collectively:  together  with  The 
Amount  of  the  Revenue  arising  to  Great-Britain  therefrom.  In 
a  Series  of  Letters,  written  originally  to  the  Right  Honourable 
the  Earl  of  *  *  *  *  *  *  *  *  By  an  Old  and  Experienced  Trader.

pp.  122.  Map  and  Plate,  ba.,  c,  h.  +  [Reprinted,  n.  p.] 
1770.    i2mo,  pp.  89.  1379^ 

"The  author  was  the  first  to  give  accurate  intelligence  of  Hudson's  Bay,  and  to 
institute  an  enquiry  about  a  more  successful  commerce  with  the  Americans.  The 
book  is  said  to  have  been  published  under  the  auspices  of  Lord  Chatham,  and  both  the 
English  and  the  Americans,  at  that  crisis,  were  so  eager  to  possess  it,  that  it  was  bought 
and  read  by  one  party  with  the  same  avidity  that  it  was  bought  and  destroyed  by  the 
other.  —  Dibdin's  Library  Companion^  ii.  65.  See  "  M.  Rev.,"  XLi.  44.  It  was  pub- 
lished in  French,  with  the  following  title  : 

[Cluny.]  Le  Voyageur  americain,  ou  Observations  Sur 
I'Etat  actuel,  la  Culture,  le  Commerce  des  Colonies  Britanniques 
en  Amerique;  les  Exportations,  &  Importations  respectives  entre 
elles  &  la  Grande-Bretagne,  avec  un  Etat  des  revenus  que  cette 
derniere  en  retire,  &c.,  Adressees  par  un  Negociant  experimente 

en  forme  de    lettres,  au  tres-honorable    comte  de   

[Chatham]  Traduit  de  I'Anglois,  Augmente  d'un  precis  sur 
I'Amerique  Septentrionale  &  la  Republique  des  treize-£tats- 
Unis,  par  M.  Jh.  [Joseph]  iM [Mandrillon].  Amster- 
dam: J.  Schuring.  mdcclxxxii.  8vo,  pp.  viii,  i66,  3  1.  folded. 
Map.  H.  +  Amsterdam:  J.  Schuring.  M.  DCC.  Lxxxiil.  8vo, 
pp.  viij,  264.  H.  13797

COATES. 


Coast  Survey.  Annual  Report  of  the  Superintendent  of  the 
Coast  Survey,  showing  the  Progress  of  that  Work  during  the 
year  1849.  Washington.  1 850.  8vo.  Continued  annually; 
since  1852,  in  4to.  13821 

The  first  law  providing  for  the  survey  of  the  coast  of  the  United  States  was  approved 
February  lo,  1807.  The  survey  was  commenced  under  the  superintendence  of  F.  R. 
Hassler  in  18  16,  and  stopped  by  Act  of  Congress,  14th  April,  1818.  The  papers  relating 
to  these  operations  are  included  in  the  "  American  State  Papers,"  class  "  Commerce 
and  Navigation,"  in  Senate  Document  143,  15th  Congress,  1st  Session,  and  in  Vol.  11., 
New  Series,  "Transactions  of  the  American  Philosophical  Society  of  Philadelphia," 
The  law  of  1807  was  renewed  in  1832,  and  the  survey  was  recommenced  under  Mr. 
Hassler,  whose  reports  extended  to  1843,  ^"^  ^""^  followed  by  those  of  A.  D.  Bache, 
from  1844  to  1865.  The  volume  for  1866,  by  J.  E.  Hillard,  was  published  in 
1869.  These  several  reports  are  contained  in  the  Congressional  documents  of  the 
respective  sessions.  See  aho  "  N.  Am.  Rev.,"  XLii.  75;  liv.  4465  "Journal  of 
Science,"  xlix.  229;  Second  Series,  v.  3075  xii.  1585  and  "Hunt's  Magazine," 
XXI.  266.

ments. ...  [From  the  New- York  Times,  n.  p.  1858.]  8vo,

[Cobbett.]  Porcupine's  Works;  containing  various  Writ- 
ings and  Selections,  exhibiting  a  Faithful  Picture  of  the  United 
States  of  America;  of  their  Governments,  Laws,  Politics,  and 
Resources;  of  the  Characters  of  their  Presidents,  Governors, 
Legislators,  Magistrates,  and  Military  Men;  and  of  the  Cus- 
toms, Manners,  Morals,  Religion,  Virtues  and  Vices  of  the 
People:  comprising  also  a  Complete  Series  of  Historical  Docu- 
ments and  Remarks,  from  the  End  of  the  War,  in  1783,  to  the 
Election  of  the  President,  in  March,  1801.  By  William  Cob- 
bett. ...  London:  Cobbett  and  Morgan.  May.,  1 80 1.  12  vols., 
8vo,  pp.  400;  2  1.,  pp.  472;  2  1.,  pp.  440;  2  1.,  pp.  444;  2 
I.,  pp.  432;  2  1.,  pp.  432;  2  1.,  pp.  430;  2  1.,  pp.  480;  2  1., 
pp.  412;   2  1.,  pp.  449,  3;   2  1.,  pp.  434;   2  1.,  pp.  252,  81. 

"Cobbett,  in  these  volumes,  has  left  a  picture  of  the  politics  and  leading  politicians 
of  America,  which  (with  caution)  must  be  studied  by  all  who  would  understand  the 
party  questions  which  agitated  America,  and  the  violence  with  which  they  were  dis- 
cussed."—  Kent.

Part  I.  was  issued  separately:  '^  Neiv  York:  Clayton  &  Kingdand.  l8i8."  i6mo, 
pp.  134,  Also:  An  Address  to  the  People  of  England.  By  William  Cobbett. 
Philadelphia.  i8l2.  8vo.  p.  —  Anti-Galican,  or  the  Lover  of  his  own  Country.  ... 
Philadelphia.  1797.  8vo,  pp.  82.  —  Cobbett's  Weekly  Register,  for  Dec.  13,  1823, 
contains  a  Letter  to  Mr.  Wilberforce,  on  his  "  Appeal  in  behalf  of  Negro  Slaves," 
&c.  London.  —  "A  History  of  the  American  Jacobins,  Philadelphia.  1796,"  is  added 
to  Playfair's  "Jacobinism."  Concerning  Cobbett,  see  Sir  H.  Lytton  Bulwer's  Historical 
Characters,  London,  1868,  Vol.  11.  "William  Cobbett,  a  voluminous  political  writer, 
was  the  son  of  a  farmer  and  publican  at  Farnham,  in  Surrey,  England.  He  ran  away 
from  his  father,  enlisted  as  a  soldier,  and  served  for  some  time  in  Nova  Scotia  and  New 
Brunswick.  Returning  to  England,  he  received  his  discharge,  and  came  to  New  York 
in  1792.  In  1796  he  settled  in  Philadelphia,  and,  establishing  'Peter  Porcupine's 
Gazette,'  took  a  lively  interest  in  the  political  questions  of  the  day."  "  He  (Cobbett) 
is  a  man  filled  with  odium  humani  generis.  His  malevolence  and  lying  are  beyond 
anything."  —  Jeremy  Bentham.  "The  general  characteristics  of  his  style  were  pers- 
picuity unequalled  and  inimitable;  a  homely,  muscular  vigor,  a  purity,  always  simple, 
and  a  raciness,  often  elegant."  —  London  Times.  The  following  works  relate  to  Cob- 
bett:

[CocKiNGs  (George).]  The  American  War,  A  Poem ;  In 
Six  Books.  In  which  the  Names  of"  the  Officers  who  have

1.,  Advt.,  I  ].,  pp.  i8i.      Plan  of  Bunker's  Hill.  c.  14108 

"  A  very  remarkable  specimen  of  poetry."     Set  "  M.  Rev.,"  lxv.  469. 

CocKiNGS.  The  Conquest  of  Canada  ;  or  the  Siege  of  Que- 
bec. An  Historical  Tragedy.  Of  Five  Acts.  By  George 
Cockings.  London  :  Printed  for  the  Author,  mdcclxvi.  8vo,  pp. 
v,  76.     j.C.B.     +  Albany.    1773.    8vo.  bm.  14109 

A  rare  and  curious  production.  The  following  extract  will  give  some  idea  of  the 
poetry.  The  Lady  Abbess  at  Quebec  is  comforting  her  nuns,  who  dread  falling  into 
the  hands  of  the  English;  she  reassures  them,  and  says  : 

"  What  the  whole  Nation  is  I 

Cannot  say,  but  I'm  told  by  a  Lady, 
Who  was  at  Louisburg  taken  by  them, 
That  the  officers  behaved  with  the  greatest 
Civility  and  Politeness  to  all, 
But  in  a  more  peculiar  manner,  to 
The  Religious  Ladies  and  Orders,  of 
All  sorts ;   kept  the  strictest  decorum  in 
The  Town,  among  the  Soldiers,  and  stuck  most 
Honorably  to  their  Capitulation,  &c. 
ind  Nun.   O  !   terrible,  —  if  they  should  take  the  City  — 
And  we  should  fall  into  the  hands  of  those 
Rough  Englishmen  !  "  &c. 

Cookings.  War :  An  Heroic  Poem,  from  the  Taking  of 
Minorca  by  the  French  to  the  Reduction  of  the  Havannah,  to

Cockings.  War  ;  an  Heroic  Poem.  From  the  taking  of

books  include  the  operations  of  the  contending  nations  both  in  Europe  and  America. 
This  poem  was  popular  in  its  day,  and  passed  through  four  editions.  Cockings  was 
also  the  author  of"  Stentorian  Eloquence  and  Medical  Infallibility,"  a  satire,  in  verse, 
on  itinerant  preachers,  published  in  1771,  and  of  "Benevolence  and  Gratitude,"  a 
poem,  published  in  1773.  "The  American  Times.  A  Satire,"  has  also  been 
attributed  to  him.      See  our  No.  1244,  and   Duyckinck,  i.  432.

Latter  is  discoursed,  how  the  Navigation  of  England  may  be

Brome^l^c.    1675.    4to,  pp.  115.  14241 

The  author's  theory  was,  that  the  emigration  to  the  colonies  was  injurious  to  Eng- 
land. See  McCulloch's  "Lit.  Pol.  Econ.,"  p.  40.  See  also  his  "Treatises,  wherein  is 
demonstrated,  that  the  Church  and  State  of  England,  are  in  equal  Danger  with  the 
Trade  of  it  —  and  from  what  Causes  the  Dutch  Govern  and  Manage  Trade  better  than 
the  English."

[CoLDEN  (Cadwallader).]  An  |  Explication  |  of  the  \  First 
Causes  I  of  I  Action  In  Matter,  |  And,  |  Of  the  |  Cause  of  Gravi- 
tation. I  New-Tork:  \  Printed  By  James  Parker^  1745.  I  Sm. 
8vo,  pp.  vi,  43,  (l).  14267 

This  edition  is  a  volume  of  the  greatest  rarity.  I  have  seen  but  one  copy,  which 
belongs  to  Mr.  William  Menzies.  The  dedication  to  James  Alexander,  Esq.,  at  New 
York,  is  dated,  **  Coddingham,  in  the  Province  of  New  York,  Dec.  lo.  1745,"  and 
signed  Cadwallader  Golden.  Concerning  its  rarity,  see  Jefferson's  letter  to  Francis 
Hopkinson,  in  1786.  See  also  Sparks'  "Franklin,"  vi.  95,  117,  and  "M.  Rev.,"  vii. 
459  5  IX.  2035  XXI.  397,  500.  The  first  English  edition  was  printed  without  the 
author's  knowledge  or  consent.  The  second  English  edition  was  enlarged.  Buffon 
caused  it  to  be  translated  into  French,  and  there  was  a  German  translation  published 
at  Hamburg  or  Leipzig,  or  both,  which  I  have  not  seen. 

CoLDEN.  An  Explication  of  the  First  Causes  of  Action  in 
Matter  ;  and  of  the  Cause  of  Gravitation.  By  Cadwallader 
Colden.  New-Tork:  Printed.  And  London:  Reprinted  for  J. 
Brindley  ...  1746.  8vo,  pp.  75.  p.  -f-  Second  Edition.  Lon- 
don:  J.  Dodsley.    1751.  14268

Action  in   Matter,  the   Gravitation    in    Bodies  and   the   Motion   of  Planets,   explained

from  those  Principles.  London.  1752."  4to.  Its  title  hardly  belongs  to  our  place, 
seeing  that  it  does  not  relate  to  America  5  but  the  work  is  eagerly  sought  after  by 
American  collectors,  and,  at  the  time  of  its  publication,  excited  much  comment.

A  volume  of  the  greatest  rarity,  not  more  than  six  copies  being  known  in  the  United 
States.  For  some  interesting  particulars  concerning  the  author,  see  Dr.  Shea's  intro- 
duction to  his  reprint  of  this  edition.  The  work  was  occasioned  by  a  dispute  between 
the  government  of  New  York  and  some  merchants,  and  is  mainly  a  legal  argument. 

Colden.  The  History  of  the  Five  Indian  Nations  of  the 
Province  of  New  York.  By  Cadwallader  Colden.  Reprinted 
exactly  from  Bradford's  New  York  Edition,  (1727.)  With  an 
Introduction  and  Notes,  by  John  Gilmary  Shea.  New  York:  T. 
H.  Morrell.  1866.  8vo,  pp.  xl,  I  1.,  pp.  xvii,  (i),  141.  Portrait 
and  Map.  1427 1 

Of  this  reprint,  loo  copies  were  printed  in  8vo,  and  25  copies  in  imp.  8vo.  "Not- 
withstanding the  high  position  of  Gov.  Colden,  the  many  offices  he  filled,  and  the 
great  service  he  rendered  the  colony  of  New  York,  no  biography  of  him  was  ever 
written.  Mr.  Shea,  in  his  introduction,  has  now  performed  this  duty,  and  has  done  it 
well."  —  Bartlett.  The  following  work  is,  in  fact,  necessary  to  complete  the  first 
edition  :

GOLDEN.  223 

CoLDEN.  The  History  of  the  Five  Indian  Nations  of  Canada, 
which  are  dependent  on  the  Province  of  New-York  in  America, 
and  are  the  Barrier  between  the  English  and  French  in  that  Part 
of  the  World.  With  Accounts  of  their  Religion,  Manners, 
Customs,  Laws,  and  Forms  of  Government;  their  several  Bat- 
tles and  Treaties  with  the  European  Nations;  particular  Rela- 
tions of  their  several  Wars  with  the  other  Indians ;  and  a  true 
Account  of  the  present  State  of  our  Trade  with  them.  In  which 
are  shewn  the  great  Advantage  of  their  Trade  and  Alliance  to 
the  British  Nation,  and  the  Intrigues  and  Attempts  of  the  French 
to  eno;ao;e  them  from  us;  a  Subject  nearly  concerning  all  our 
American  Plantations,  and  highly  meriting  the  Consideration  of 
the  British  Nation  at  this  Juncture.  By  the  Honourable  Cad- 
wallader  Colden,  Esq;  One  of  his  Majesty's  Counsel,  and  Sur- 
veyor-General of  New  York.  To  which  are  added.  Accounts 
of  the  several  other  Nations  of  Indians  in  North  America,  their 
Numbers,  Strength,  &;c.  and  the  Treaties  which  have  been 
lately  made  with  them.  A  Work  highly  entertaining  to  all,  and 
particularly  useful  to  the  Persons  who  have  any  Trade  or  Concern 
in  that  Part  of  the  World.  London:  T.  Osborne,  mdccxlvii. 
8vo,  pp.  20,  204,  283.  BA.,  c.  14273 

Colden  dedicated  his  work  to  Gov.  Burnet,  and  his  dedication  explains  itself;  in  this 
London  edition,  Burnet  gives  place  to  Gen.  Oglethorpe,  and  the  dedication  becomes 
nonsense.  The  English  editor  also  shows  an  antipathy  to  Indian  names,  suppressing 
them  habitually,  striking  out  important  passages,  and,  instead  of  the  speeches  which 
Colden  gives  at  length,  substitutes  meagre  abridgments.  In  fact,  the  whole  work  is 
so  cut  up  and  altered,  that  the  reader  of  the  English  edition  cannot  be  sure  that  he  is 
quoting  Colden  at  all.  Still  this  edition  is  desirable,  as  it  contains  many  public  docu- 
ments.    The  following  is  the  same  work,  with  a  new  title-page  only : 

Colden.  The  History  ot  the  Five  Indian  Nations  of  Canada, 
which  are  the  Barrier  between  the  English  and  French  in  that 
Part  of  the  World.  With  particular  Accounts  of  their  Religion, 
Manners,  Customs,  Laws,  and  Government;  their  several  Bat- 
tles and  Treaties  with  the  European  Nations;  their  Wars  with 
the  other  Indians;  and  a  true  Account  of  the  present  State  of 
our  Trade  with  them.  In  which  are  shewn,  the  great  Advantage 
of  their  Trade  and  Alliance  to  the  British  Nation ;  and  the 
Intrigues  and  Attempts  of  the  French  to  engage  them  from  us  ; 
a  Subject  nearly  concerning  all  our  American  Plantations,  and 
highly  meriting  the  Consideration  of  the  British  Nation.  By  the 
Honourable  Cadwallader  Colden,  Esq;  One  of  His  Majesty's 
Counsel,  and  Surveyor-General  of  New-York.  To  which  are 
added,   Accounts   of  the   several   other   Nations   of  Indians   in

These  collections  contain  the  documents  relative  to  the  expulsion  of  the  Jesuits  from 
the  Spanish  dominions,  and  the  confiscation  of  their  property,  including  the  names  of 
the  colleges  and  houses  of  the  order,  the  application  of  the  confiscated  property,  &c. 
A  complete  series  consists  of  five  parts,  which  are  rarely  found  together.  Part  v.  was 
published  in  1784.     Our  collation  is  of  parts  i.  to  in.  only.

A  Collection  of  State  Papers  relative  to  the  War  against 
France  now  carrying  on  by  Great  Britain  and  the  several  other 
European  powers,  containing  authentic  copies  of  Treaties,  Con- 
ventions, Proclamations,  Manifestos,  Declarations,  Memorials, 
Remonstrances,  Official  Letters,  Parliamentary  Papers,  London 
Gazette  Accounts  of  the  War,  &c.  Many  of  which  have  never 
before  been  published  in  England.  London:  J,  Dehrett.  1794. 
3  vols.,  8vo,  pp.  iv,  344,  171,  7  1.;  pp.  484,  168,  6  1.;  pp.  iv, 
376,  48.  J.c.B.  14389 

"  The  object  of  the  editor  of  this  work  has  been  to  collect  all  State  Papers  illustrative 
of  the  sentiments,  actions,  and  views  of  the  European  powers,  in  the  present  war  against 
France,  and  also  of  the  United  States  of  America,  who,  though  situated  in  another 
quarter  of  the  globe,  are,  notwithstanding,  by  language,  customs,  and  connexions,  so 
intimately  woven  with  the  European  system  that  their  conduct  is  more  important  to  it 
than  that  of  many  of  the  European  states  themselves."  —  Introduction.

COLLECTION. 


243 


cussion  of  the  question  of  Protestant  bishops  in  the  colonies.  By  the  "Advertise- 
ment" on  the  reverse  of  the  title-page,  it  appears  that  a  half-sheet  of  these  papers 
was  printed  weekly,  and  served  to  the  patrons  of  the  "  New  York  Thursday's  Journal," 
without  additional  charge.  The  "  Advertisement "  at  the  end  of  the  second  volume 
calls  upon  the  subscribers  to  pay  up  to  enable  him  to  "  proceed  with  the  third  volume." 
It  is  a  tolerably  fair  resume  of  both  sides  of  the  queuion,  which  was  agitated  with  con- 
siderable warmth.      The  work  is  very  scarce.      1  know  of  the  sale  of  one  copy  only.

Collot.  Voyage  dans  I'Amerique  septentrionale,  ou  de- 
scription des  pays  arroses  par  le  Mississipi,  TOhio,  le  Missouri 
et  autres  rivieres  affluentes;  observations  exactes  sur  le  cours  et 
les  sondes  de  ces  rivieres;  sur  les  villes,  villages  hameaux  et 
fermes  de  cette  partie  du  Nouveau-Monde;  suivi  de  remarques 
philosophiques,  politiques,  militaires  et  commerciales ;  et  d'un 
projet  de  ligues  frontieres  et  de  limites  generales.  Avec  un 
atlas  de  36  cartes,  plans,  vues  et  figures.  Par  feu  le  General 
Collot  ...  .  Paris:  Arthus  Bertrand.  1826.  2  vols.,  8vo,  Atlas 
in  4to,  Title,  pp.  iv,  viij,  416;  Title,  pp.  427,  36  1.  Maps 
and  Plates.  c,  h.  14460 

"This  work  was  printed  both  in  French  and  English,  but  not  published,  at  the 
time  of  General  CoUot's  death,  which  happened  in  1805.  More  than  twenty  years 
afterwards,  the  whole  impression  came  into  the  hands  of  M.  Bertrand,  ar.  eminent 
publisher  in  Paris,  v/ho  reserved  one  hundred  copies  of  the  English  and  three  hundred 
of  the  French  edition,  and  made  waste  paper  of  the  remainder.  The  translation  was 
made  by  an  English  gentleman,  under  the  eyes  of  the  general  himself.  The  journey 
was  undertaken  in  1796,  at  the  request  of  Adet,  minister  from  France  to  the  United 
States,  for  the  purpose  of  obtaining  'a  minute  detail  of  the  political,  commercial  and 
military  state  of  the  western  part  of  that  continent.'  An  appendix  is  referred  to,  but 
appears  to  have  been  printed  only  in  the  French  edition.  It  relates  to  the  general's 
dispute  with  the  Spanish  Governor  of  Louisiana.  The  maps  and  plates  are  beautifully 
engraved  by  Tardieu.  A  few  copies  were  printed  on  large  vellum  paper." — Rich. 
The  following  is  the  English  title  : 

Collot.  A  Journey  in  North  America,  containing  a  Sur- 
vey of  the  Countries  watered  by  the  Mississippi,   Ohio,   Mis- 


252  COLMAN. 

souri,  and  other  Affluing  Rivers;  with  Exact  Observations  on 
the  Course  and  Soundings  of  these  Rivers;  and  on  the  Towns, 
Villages,  Hamlets,  and  Farms  of  that  Part  of  the  New  World  ; 
followed  by  Philosophical,  Political,  Military  and  Commercial  Re- 
marks, and  by  a  Projected  Line  of  Frontiers  and  General  Limits. 
Illustrated  by  an  Atlas  of  36  Maps,  etc.  By  Gen.  V.  Callot. 
late  in  the  French  Service,  and  Governor  of  Guadeloupe.  Paris:

of  36  Maps  and  Plates.  14461

Jacob  Aertiz  Colom.    1669."     It  is  apparently  the  same  as  the  following  : 

Colom.  Nova  totius  Terrarum  Orbis  Geographica  ac  Hy- 
drographica  Tabula.  Amstelodami,  [1663.]  Folio,  Engraved 
Title,  and  23  folded  Maps.  ^4549

Stevens.

"  Complete  copies  of  this  important  work  are  extremely  scarce.  '  A  publicaticn, 
such  as  no  other  country  of  South  America  can  boast  of,  is  the  Coleccion  de  Docu- 
mentos relativos  a  la  vida  de  Simon  Bolivar.  Caracas.  1826-33.  22  vols.,  of  which 
only  one  copy  (that  in  the  Library  of  Darmstadt)  is  to  be  found  in  Germany.'  (Ger- 
vinus,  Geschichte  des  19.  Jahrhunderts.  Vol.  in.,  p.  166.)  We  may  add,  that 
there  is  another  copy  belonging  to  the  Bibliotheque  de  Ste.  Genevieve,  of  Paris  5  but 
these  two  copies  seem  to  be  the  only  ones  which  have  hitherto  reached  Europe."  —

Colombo  (Cristoforo),  the  Discoverer  of  America ;  born  in 
Genoa^  1435?  ^^^^  ''^  ^506. 

Mr.  Harrisse,  in  his  "  Notes  on  Columbus,"  and  his  "  Bibliotheca  Americana 
Vetustissima,"  has  discussed  the  bibliography  of  Columbus  and  his  writings  at  so  much 
length,  and  with  so  much  industry,  that  we  shall  content  ourselves,  generally,  with  a 
reprint  of  the  titles  he  has  given  ;  submitting  them,  however,  to  the  revision  of  those 
American  collectors  who  are  so  fortunate  as  to  possess  the  works  described.  Mr.  R. 
H.  Major's  "Bibliography"  prefixed  to  the  second  edition  of  the  Select  Letters  of  Co- 
lumbus has  also  been  used.  His  titles  differ  slightly  from  Harrisse's.  We  need  scarcely 
remark  that  these  letters  of  Columbus  are  all  of  the  highest  degree  of  rarity,  and  will 
range  in  value  from  $2.50  each  to  any  amount  which  the  competition  of  any  two 
wealthy  collectors  would  realize. 

Colombo.  Epistola  Christofori  Colom :  cui  ^etas  nostra 
multu  debet:  dejlnsulis  Indite  supra  Gangem  nuper  inuentis. 
Ad  quas  perqui-]  rendas  octauo  antea  mense  auspicijs  &  ^ere  inu- 
ictissimi  Fernan-|di  Hispaniarum  Regis  missus  fuerat:  ad  Mag- 
nificum  dnm  Ra|phaelem  Sanxis :  eiusdem  serenissimi  Regis

Maij.  M.cccc.xciij.  IPontificatus  Alexandri  Sexti  Anno  Primo.  | 
Sm.  4to.  14628 

Four  leaves,  containing  thirty-four  lines  in  a  full  page,  without  date  or  place.  This 
celebrated  letter  of  Columbus  is  the  first  printed  document  relative  to  America,  and 
appears  to  have  been  originally  written  in  Spanish.  Mr.  S.  L.  M.  Barlow  possesses 
the  only  known  copy  in  the  United  States.  The  only  other  copies  known  are  in  the 
British  Museum  [2  copies]  and  the  Royal  Library  at  Munich.

Primo.|      Sm.   8vo,    10   leaves   of  27   lines   to   a  full   page.      8 
Woodcuts.  L.  14629 

With  a  portrait  of  Ferdinand  on  the  recto  of  the  tenth  leaf,  and  coat-of-arms  of 
Granada  on  the  verso.  Mr.  Lenox  possesses  the  only  perfect  copy  known.  Two 
other  copies,  both  lacking  the  tenth  leaf,  are  known,  one  in  the  British  Museum,  and 
one  which  is  said  to  have  been  stolen  from  the  Ambrosian  Library  at  Milan.  This 
edition  was  supposed  to  have  been  reprinted  in  Paris  by  Pilinski  about  18585  but,  on 
examination,  the  reprint  was  found  to  lack  the  first  and  last  leaves.

parisius  in  capo  gaillardi.\  [1493.]  4^^>  4  leaves,  39  lines  to  a 
full  page.  14632 

Tiie  title  in  three  large  black  letter  lines  on  the  recto  of  the  first  of  the  four 
leaves,  the  verso  contains  the  epigram  and  the  woodcut  representing  the  angel 
appearing  to  the  shepherds,  etc.  This  edition  is  precisely  the  same  in  every  respect, 
and  from  the  same  type  as  No.  14633  ;  it  has  a  subscription  at  the  end,  "  Christ- 
oforus  Colom  Oceane  classis  Prefectus,"  which  is  omitted  in  the  other.  Mr.  J.  C. 
Brown,  of  Providence,  possesses  the  only  known  copy.     See  [Harrisse],  pp.  20,  21. 

Colombo.  Epistola  de  insulis  noui  |  ter  repertis  Impressa 
parisius  In  campo  gaillardi.  |  [Vignette.  Second  leaf  begins] 
Epistola  Christofori  Co|lom:  cui  etas  nostra,  multu  debet:  de  In- 


276  COLOMBO. 

sulls  indie  supra  Gangem  |  nuper  inuentis.  Ad  quas  per  quiredas 
octauo  antea  mense  au|spicijs  &  ere  inulctissimi  Fernandi  His- 
paniarum  Regis  missus  |  fuerat :  ad  magnificu  diiz  Raphaelem

ac  I/V/^ratus  vir  Aliader  |  de  Cosco  ab  Hispano  ideomate  in  latinu 
conuertit:  tercio  kl'slMaij.  M.cccc.xciij.  Potificatus  Alexadri. 
vi.  Anno  pvimo  :\  Impressa  parlssius  in  Campo  G^/7/^r^/.  |[i493.] 
4  1.,  39  lines  on  a  full  page.  '4^33 

"  The  title  is  on  the  recto  of  the  first  leaf  over  a  woodcut  device  of  the  printer,  Guiot 
Marchant,  and  on  the  verso  is  the  usual  epigram  over  another  woodcut,  representing 
the  angel  appearing  to  the  shepherds.  The  title|of  the  letter  succeeds  on  the  top  of  the 
recto  of  the  second  leaf  (a  ij)  and  the  text,  in  black  letter,  fills  the  next  three  leaves. 
Mr.  Brown  possesses  the  only  known  copy  in  the  United  States.  There  is  one  in  the 
Bodleian  Library,  at  Oxford,  another  at  Gottingen.  Five  copies  have  been  made  in 
facsimile,  by  John  Harris,  Sen.,  from  the  copy  in  the  Bodleian  Library.  These  six  works 
represent  the  various  known  editions  of  the  Latin  translation  of  the  letter  addressed 
by  Columbus  to  Raphael  Sanchez  on  his  return  in  1493.  Mr.  Major  considers 
that  the  order  of  publication  was  (our)  14630,  31,  28,  29,  32,  33."  Only  one  of  those 
six  editions  contains  the  name  of  the  printer,  his  place  of  residence,  and  the  year 
in  which  it  was  printed.  It  is  our  No.  1463 1.  Another  has  the  printer's  name 
and  residence,  but  no  date  (No,  14633)  ;  whilst  a  third  (No.  14632)  gives  only 
the  place.  The  other  three  (Nos.  14628,  14629,  and  14630)  are  all  iine  anno  aut 
loco.  The  text,  with  the  exception  of  variations  in  the  use  of  contracted  letters,  is  the 
same  for  all.  The  titles  differ.  In  Nos.  14628,  14629,  14632,  and  14633,  the  name 
of  King  Ferdinand  only  is  given  5  in  Nos.  14630,  and  14631,  that  of  gueen  Isabella 
is  added.  In  Nos.  14628,  14629,  14632,  and  14633,  the  recipient  of  the  letter  is 
called  Raphael;  in  Nos.  14630  and  14631,  he  is  named  Gabriel.  His  family  name  is 
spelled  in  Nos.  14628,  14629,  14632,  and  14633,  Sanxis;  in  No.  1^6-^0,  Sanctis ; 
in  No.  14631,  SancAes.  The  translator  is  mentioned  as  Aliander  in  Nos.  14628, 
14629,  14632,  and  14633;  in  Nos.  14630  and  14631,  as  Leander ;  whilst  No.  14631 
adds  the  complimentary  adjective  of  ger.erosus  where  all  the  others  have  nobilis.  No. 
14629  is  the  only  one  which  lacks  the  words  "Indie  supra  Gangem"  in  the  title.  Nos. 
14632  and  14633  were  certainly  printed  at  Paris,  and  No.  1463 1  at  Rome,  which  is 
said,  by  Morelli,  Gianorini,  Bossi,  and  others,  to  be  also  the  case  with  Nos.  14628, 
146x9,  and  14630.  There  are  no  positive  proofs  that  the  latter  are  Roman  impres- 
sions, but  the  probability  is  that  they  were  at  least  published  in  Italy.  The  type  used 
in  Nos.  14628,  14629,  and  14630  differ  from  each  other  in  size,  form,  and  in  the  employ- 
ment of  contractions.  We  suppose,  therefore,  that  they  were  printed  by  three  different 
printers.  Frank  Silber,  usually  called  Eucharius  Argenteus  or  Argyrios,  is  certainly  the 
printer  of  No.  14630,  Guyot  Marchant  or  Mercator  that  of  Nos.  14632  and  14633. 
Nos.  14628  and  1463 1  have  the  same  type,  and  are,  therefore,  the  work  of  one  printer, 
who  is  supposed,  from  the  great  similarity  found  to  exist  between  the  latter  and  the 
books  printed  in  1493  ^Y  Stephanus  Plannck,  to  be  the  work  of  that  excellent  typo- 
grapher." Washington  Irving,  in  his  life  of  Columbus,  is  very  eloquent  in  praise  of  the 
language  used  in  this  first  document  ever  printed  relative  to  the  discovery  of  America, 
and  he  considers  it,  on  the  whole,  as  far  more  authentic  and  valuable  than  the  subse- 
quent works  of  other  authors,  who,  for  the  most  part,  wrote  of  things  they  never  saw, 
and  who  recorded  as  facts  circumstances  and  deeds  which  were  entirely  fictitious,  and 
existed  only  in  their  own  imaginations.  All  other  writers,  since  the  discovery  of  these 
precious  gems,  unite  in  admiration  and  the  warmest  eulogiums  of  their  merit  and 
extraordinary  rarity,  and  some  state,  "  Every  word  of  the  letter  should  be  incorporated 
in  all  future  histories  of  America."  Dr.  Robertson,  notwithstanding  his  research,  was 
totally  unacquainted  with  their  existence.

Title  from  Harrisse,  who  describes  the  copy  in  the  Ambrosian  Library.  "  While  still 
on  board  his  caravel,  February  1 5th,  off  the  island  of  St.  Mary,  Columbus  wrote  two  otiicial 
accounts  of  his  voyage,  one  of  which  was  addressed  to  Raphael  or  Gabriel  Sanchez  or 
Sanxis,  the  Crown  Treasurer.  No  copy,  either  in  print  or  in  manuscript,  of  the 
Spanish  original,  has  yet  been  found,  but  the  discovery  made  a  few  years  ago,  in  the 
Ambrosian  Library,  of  a  printed  copy  oi  tJie  letter  addressed  to  Luiz  de  Santangel  (No. 
14640),  warrants  the  belief  that  not  only  it  may  have  been  printed,  but  that  it  is  not 
irretrievably  lost.  As  to  the  original  itself,  notwithstanding  the  diligent  searches  in- 
stituted by  Munoz  in  Simancas,  and  Navarrete  in  the  Lotija  at  Seville,  where,  after  the 
establishment  of  the  General  Archives  of  the  Indies,  in  1792,,  all  documents  relating 
to  the  V^estern  World  had  been  transferred,  no  traces  of  it  have  ever  been  discovered. 
The  Spanish  text  of  the  letter  to  Santangel  had  been  made  known  through  Navar- 
rette,  who  possessed  a  transcript  from  the  original,  which,  in  18 18,  was  still  deposited 
among  the  archives  at  Simancas.  It  was  not  known  then,  or  even  suspected,  that 
this  important  document  had  been  printed  before.  This  rariisime  plaquette  is  printed 
in  a  kind  of  semi-gothic  type,  of  the  roughest  character,  resembling  none  of  the  in- 
cunabula which  we  have  been  able  to  examine.  The  fact  alone  that  the  text  is  in 
the  Spanish  language  authorizes  the  belief  that  it  was  printed  in  Spain.  The  text  of 
the  Ambrosian  plaquette  was  first  published  three  yi-.rs  ago.  It  is  also  in  two  late 
American  publications.  The  Marquis  D'Adda  has  lately  reproduced  the  entire  letter 
in  facsimile."  —  Harrisse,  pp.  6,  24,  25,  and  2.7.

Colombo.  Copia  de  la  Lettera  per  Columbo  mandata  a  11 
Serenissimi  Re  et  Regina  di  Spagna:  de  le  insule  et  luoghi  per 
lui  trouate.  [Verso:]  Constantio  Bayuera  Bressaiio|Al  Mag- 
nifico  et  Clarissimo  Francesco  |  Bragadeno  Podesta  di  Bressa  S. 
[Colophon :]  Stampata  in  Venetia  {a  nome  de  Constantio  Bayuera 
citadano  di  Bressa)^  per  Simone  de  Louere.  a  di  7  di  Maxo,  1505. 
cum  priuilegio.     Sm.  4to,  8  1.,  the  last  blank.  J4642 

"  Prompted  by  the  success  of  Vasco  da  Gama's  voyage,  Columbus,  fitting  out  a  new 
expedition,  sailed,  taking  with  him  his  brother  Bartholomew  and  his  own  son  and  future 
historian,  Fernando,  May  9th,  1502,  from  Cadiz,  with  four  small  caravels.  After  a 
voyage  of  only  twenty-five  days,  the  Admiral  reached  what  is  supposed  to  be  the  island 
now  called  Martinique;  discovered,  July  30th,  the  unimportant  Island  of  Bonacca, 
near  the  Bay  of  Honduras;  ...  The  above  work  is  a  description  of  the  events  of  this 
voyage  (which  was  Columbus'  fourth  and  last),  only  to  July  7th,  1503,  and  is  dated 
from  Jamaica.  It  was  originally  written  in  Spanish.  ...  We  have  the  authority  of 
Pinelo  for  the  assertion  that  it  was  printed;  but  no  such  Spanish  edition  has  yet  been 
found.  Aa  Italian  translation,  however,  either  from  that  printed  original  or  from  a 
MS.,  was  published  in  Italy  very  soon  after  the  return  of  Columbus;  it  is  the  present 
No.  14642.  The  latter  had  long  been  forgotten  when  Morelli,  the  modest,  obliging 
and  erudite  librarian  of  the  St.  Mark  Library  at  Venice,  corrected  the  text  of  Simon  de 
Lovere's  edition,  and  republished  it  in  18 10  under  the  title  now  so  frequently  quoted 
of  'Lettera  rarissima.'  " — Harrisse.  Ste  No.  14643,  Infra.  An  English  version  of 
what  purports  to  be  Columbus'  account  of  his  fourth  voyage,  is  included  in  "  A  Mis- 
cellaneous Collection  :  consisting  of  An  Original  Letter  from  the  Fen  of  Columbus, 
never  before  published,  some  Original  Poetry,  and  detached  pieces  of  Prose;  inter- 
spersed with  a  few  neat  Selections.  The  whole  revised  and  corrected  by  a  Gentleman 
of  the  Inner  Temple.  London:  for  the  Editor.  1803."  4to,  5  1.,  pp.  132,  (4).  The 
dedication  to  this  work  is  signed  M.  H.

Colombo  (F.)  Historic  |  Del  S.  D.  Fernando  Colombo;  | 
Nelle  quali  s'ha  particolare,  &  vera  relatione  |  della  vita,  &  de'

Nuouamente  di  lingua  Spagnoula  tradotte  nell'  Italiana]dal  S. 
Alfonso  Vlloa.  |  Con  Privilegio.  \  In  Venetia^  M'D'L'ii.'X.l.\Appresso 
Francesco  de'  Franceschi  Senese.  |    i6mo.  Title,  pp.  36,  496.       ba. 

First  edition  5  very  scarce.  "The  author  was  born  at  Cordova,  in  1487,  became 
page  to  the  prince,  Don  Juan,  son  and  heir  to  Fernando  and  Isabella,  in  1494.  He 
accompanied  his  father  in  his  fourth  voyage  of  discovery  in  1502,  and,  after  the  death 
of  Columbus,  made  two  voyages  to  the  New  World.  He  also  accompanied  Charles  V 
to  Italy,  Flanders,  Germany,  &C.5  and,  after  having  travelled  over  all  Europe  and  a 
part  of  Africa  and  Asia,  died  at  Seville,  in  1539,  leaving  a  library  of  20,000  volumes, 
printed  and  manuscript,  to  the  cathedral  of  Seville.  He  was  an  eye-witness  to  the 
facts  which  he  relates  in  the  above  volume,  particularly  the  fourth  voyage  of  his  father, 
whose  papers  and  charts  he  possessed.  *  His  work  is  an  invaluable  document,  entitled 
to  great  faith,  and  is  the  corner-stone  of  the  history  of  the  American  continent.'" — ■ 
English  Catalogue.  "  Louis  Columbus,  a  person  of  debauched  character,  came  to  Genoa 
about  1568,  bringing  with  him  the  MS.  life  of  the  hero,  written  in  Spanish  by  his  son 
Don  Ferdinand,  which  he  left  in  the  hands  of  the  patrician  Fornari,  from  whom  it 
passed  to  another  patrician,  Gior.  Baptista  Marini.  The  latter,  having  gone  to  Venice, 
procured  a  translation  to  be  made  into  Italian  by  UUoa."  —  Spotorno.  "  CEttinger,  in 
his  '  Bibl.  Biogr,,'  mentions,  we  cannot  imagine  on  what  authority,  'Colombo  (Fer- 
nando), Vida  y  hechos  del  almirante  D.  Christoval  Colon,  Madr.  fol.,  1530.'  The 
learned  bibliographer  must  mean  the  wretched  translation  into  Spanish  made  for  Barcia 
(Madrid.  1747.  fol.)"  On  the  other  hand,  according  to  Barcia,  Luis  Colon  had 
intrusted  the  manuscript  to  one  Baltano  de  Fornari,  who,  notwithstanding  his  advanced 
age,  commenced  printing  the  work  simultaneously  in  the  original  Spanish,  in  Italian, 
and  Latin,  at  Venice.  If  so,  some  stray  sheets  of  Don  Fernando's  own  version  may 
yet  come  to  light.

"  Edition  inconnue  a  Ternaux.  Elle  manque  egalement  dans  la  nouvelle  edition  du 
Manuel  de  M.  Brunet,  mais  elle  est  citee  dans  le  Tresor  de  M.  Graesse."  —  Tross. 
A  curious  kind  of  chap-book  edition.  Sold  at  Puttick's,  in  i860,  for  £1  9s.  The

Colombo.  La  vie  de  Cristofle  Colomb,  et  la  decouverte 
qu'il  a  faite  des  Indes  Occidentales,  vulgairement  appellees  le

Colon  (C.)     See  Colombo  (C.) 

[Colon  (F.  M.)]  Geografia  General,  para  el  uso  de  la  Ju- 
ventud  de  Venezuela.  Caracas.  1833-37.  4  vols.,  8vo,  pp. 
544;   568;   620 ;   vi,  652,  xxviii.  14678 

With  the  exception  of  the  first  120  pages  of  the  first  volume,  the  whole  of  this  work 
relates  to  America,  and  is  principally  historical.  The  first  volume  contains  an  account 
of  St.  Domingo,  Cuba,  the  Arctic  Regions,  Canada,  and  the  United  States.  The  second 
contains  Mexico,  Central  America,  the  Antilles,  Brazil,  Buenos  Ayres,  Paraguay,  the 
Cisplatine  Republic,  Patagonia,  and  the  Islands  of  the  Pacific.  The  third  contains 
Chili,  Peru,  Bolivia,  Ecuador,  and  Nueva  Gianada.  The  fourth,  and  most  important, 
is  devoted  entirely  to  the  History  of  the  Republic  of  Venezuela,  The  author  promises 
to  give,  in  a  fifth  volume,  a  separate  account  of  each  province.  The  dedication  to 
General  Paez  is  signed  Feliciano  Montenegro  Colon,  who  says  that  "the  following 
Lessons  of  Geography,  taken  from  the  best  authors,  were  printed  in  the  Havana  in 
1826,"  referring,  probably,  to  the  first  120  pages,  the  rest  of  the  work  being  probably 
written  by  him."     The  following  is  probably   by  the  same  author: 

[Colon.]  Compendio  de  la  Historia  de  Venezuela  desde  su 
Jescubrimiento  y  conquista  hasta  que  se  declaro  estado  inde- 
pendiente.      Caracas.    1840.    8vo,  pp.  xi,  192.  14679

The  Columbian  Magazine  ;  or  Monthly  Miscellany  Con- 
taining a  View  of  the  History,  Literature,  Manners  &  Charac- 
ters of  the  Year  1 787-1 789.  ...  Philadelphia:  T.  Seddon,  W. 
Spotswood^  C.  Cist,  and  y.  Trenchard.    1786.    8vo.         ba.  14869 

An  extremely  rare  magazine.  It  commenced  with  Nov.,  1 78 6,  and  was  continued 
to  the  year  1792,  forming  altogether  twelve  volumes.  It  is  ornamented  with  numer- 
ous portraits  and  other  engravings,  principally  by  Trenchard,  who  was  one  of  the  pro- 
prietors of  the  work.  The  others  were  T.  Seddon,  Charles  Cist,  C.  Talbot,  W.  Spots- 
wood,  and  Mathew  Gary.  The  latter,  however,  from  "  the  discomfort  arising  from 
the  discordant  views  of  the  different  partners,  the  utter  improbability  of  such  a  work 
producing  any  profit  worth  the  attention  of  five  persons,  and  other  considerations,"  was 
induced  to  withdraw  from  the  concern,  and,  in  January,  1787,  commenced,  on  his 
own  account,  "The  American  Museum."      See  Vol.  i.,  No,  11  62. 

Columbian  Magazine.  June,  1798.  Kingston.,  ya?naica. 
jygS.    i2mo.  14870

The  voyages  described  are:  Vol.  i.  Candish  and  Drake  j  Raleigh  and  Keymisj  Dc 
Veer;  Houtman  ;  Van  Neck  and  Warwijck ;  De  Weert;  Van  Noort;  Both  and 
Van  Caerden  ;  Van  Neck  (2d  voyage);  Van  der  Hagen;  Harmansz  ;  Spilbergen 
and  Le  Maire.  Vol.  11.  Warwijck  and  De  Weert;  Van  der  Hagen  (2d  voyage); 
Matclief;  Van  Caerden;  Verhoeft';  Van  den  Broecke;  Van  Twist;  Spilbergen  (ist 
voyage);  L'Hermite;  Schram  and  Van  Rechteren.  Each  part  is  separately  paged,  and 
those  which  relate  to  America  are  fully  described  under  their  proper  heads  in  this 
Dictionary.  Tiele,  from  whom  I  take  the  title  and  contents,  had  never  met  with  but 
one  copy.     See  his  "  Navig.  neerland,"  pp.  9-15.

The  printed  title  of  Part  11.  is  somewhat  abridged.  Both  parts  have  also  engraved 
titles.  This  edition  omits  Candish  and  Drake,  Raleigh  and  Keymis,  but  adds  Hagc- 
naer  and  Caron.  This  edition  contains,  for  the  first  time,  an  introduction  by  Comme- 
lin,  which  gives  a  resume  of  the  Dutch  voyages  to  the  Indies  till  1631.

Common  Sense:  In  nine  Conferences,  between  a  British 
Merchant  and  a  candid  Merchant  of  America,  in  their  private 
capacities  as  friends;  tracing  the  several  causes  of  the  present 
contests  between  the  mother  country  and  her  American  subjects; 
the  fallacy  of  their  prepossessions  ;  and  the  ingratitude  and  danger 
of  them  ;  the  reciprocal  benefits  of  the  national  friendship;  and 
the  moral  obligations  of  individuals  which  enforce  it:  with  vari- 
ous anecdotes,  and  reasons  drawn  from  facts,  tending  to  con- 
ciliate all  differences,  and  establish  a  permanent  union  for  the 
common  happiness  and  glory  of  the  British  empire.  London:  J. 
Dodsley.  m,dcc,lxxv.    4to,  pp.  x,  117.  ^4998 

"The  candid  merchant  of  America,  who  is  here  employed  to  support  nine  tedious 
conferences,  and  to  be  converted  from  his  opinions,  appears  to  have  been  a  greater 
booby  than  even  the  letter-writer  who  was  brought  forward  by  a  reverend  Dean."  — 
Monthly  Review,  Lii.  261.  "Dean  Tucker  is  here  probably  referred  to."  —  Rich, 
I.  200.

It  would  not  be  difficult,  but  it  would  be  almost  useless,  to  continue  this  list  of  the 
acts  of  the  different  sessions  of  each  successive  Congress;  let  it  suffice  to  remark  that 
they  are  continuous,  and,  with  the  increased  number  and  importance  of  the  states,  have 
become  extremely  voluminous.  Tolerably  complete  sets  are  in  the  Congressional 
Library,  in  the  Boston  Public  Library,  and  the  Libraries  of  the  Boston  Athenaeum,  and 
of  Harvard  College,  and  many  of  our  stitc  libraries  contnm  portions  of  sets.

These  executive  documents  are  published  at  each  session,  and  sometimes  extend  to 
fifteen  volumes  in  one  session.  With  much  that  is  ephemeral,  there  are  to  be  found 
many  documents  of  the  first  importance  to  the  historical  student.  An  extensive  series  is 
included  in  the  Library  of  Congress,  and  in  the  Boston  Public  Library  5  the  latter  has 
published  a  useful  index  to  their  contents.     Occasionally  a  volume  is  issued  in  4to.

These  "Documents"  are  issued  at  each  session  of  the  Congress,  and,  after  the 
Twenty-ninth  Congress,  are  called  "  House  Miscellaneous  Documents."  They  consist 
of  such  papers  as  are  printed  for  the  use  of  the  members,  and  cover  many  topics  of 
historical  importance.

The  petition  sets  forth  the  grievances  of  the  colonies,  and  then  says :  "  To  a  Sovereign,

justify  the  loyal  subjects  who  fly  to  the  foot  of  his  throne  and  implore  his  clemency  for 
protection  against  them."

The  Two  Congresses  Cut  Up:  or  a  Few  Remarks  upon 
some  of  the  Votes  and  Resolutions  of  the  Continental  Congress, 
held  at  Philadelphia,  in  September,  and  the  Provincial  Congress, 
held  at  Cambridge,  in  November  1774.  By  a  Friend  to  Peace 
and  good  Order.  Boston.^  Printed.  New  York:  Reprinted  by 
'James  Livingston.   [1774.]    8vo,  pp.  14.  ^5597 

The  writer  of  this  tract  was  evidently  a  royalist.  He  thinks  that  the  opposition 
made  by  the  colonies  to  the  tax  laid  upon  tea  "is  inconsistent  with  our  profession  of 
Christianity,  with  the  loyalty  we  owe  to  our  sovereign,  and  the  reverence  and  respect 
that  is  due  to  the  British  Parliament;  and,  consequently,  instead  of  being  a  means  to 
obtain  a  repeal  of  the  acts,  will  bring  on  us  a  heavier  condemnation  than  that  of  the 
Port  Bill,  an  act  for  the  alteration  of  our  Constitution." 

What  think  ye  of  Congress  now-f*  1775.  See  [Chandler 
(Thomas  B.)],  Vol.  in..  No.  11882,  and  [Cooper  (Myles)].

The  Connecticut  Gore  Title,  stated  and  considered,  showing 
the  Right  of  the  Proprietors,  to  the  Lands  lately  purchased  by 
them,  from  the  State  of  Connecticut:  lying  West  of  the  Dela- 
ware River.  Hartford:  Printed  by  Hudson  ^  Goodwin.  1799. 
8vo,  pp.  80.  BA.  15681 

The  "Connecticut  Gore"  was  a  strip  of  land  along  the  western  part  of  the  south 
New  York  State  line,  and,  like  the  "  Connecticut  Reserve,"  was  claimed  by  Con- 
necticut under  the  old  English  grants  that  sever  the  Atlantic  seaboard  states  clear 
through  to  the  Pacific  Ocean.

This  tract  has  reference  to  the  Parliamentary  inquiry  into  the  conduct  of  the  army 
under  General  Howe.  The  writer  thinks  the  colonies  may  be  subdued.  That  not 
one-fifth  of  the  colonists  desire  a  separation  from  Great  Britain,  and  that  it  is  disgraceful 
for  any  Englishman  to  propose  an  acknowledgment  of  American  independence.  See 
"M.  Rev.,'*  Lxi.  230.

duct of  the  Minister  who  planned  it.  London:  W,  NicolL 
MDCCLXvi.    8vo,  pp.  38.  B.  15961 

The  author  recommends  the  repeal  of  the  Stamp  Act  as  the  only  means  to  reconcile 
the  colonists  to  their  mother  country,  and  to  restore  peace,  plenty,  and  cordiality  to 
every  part  of  the  British  empire.     See  "M.  Rev.,**  xxxiv.  65.

Considerations  on  the  Bill  for  a  General  Naturalization,  as  it 
may  conduce  to  the  improvement  of  our  Manufactures  and 
Traffic,  and  to  the  Strengthening  or  endangering  the  Constitu- 
tion, exemplified  in  the  revolutions  that  have  happened  in  this 
Kingdom  by  inviting  over  Foreigners  to  settle  amongst  us. 
With  an  Enquiry  into  the  nature  of  the  British  Constitution, 
and  the  Freedom  or  Servitude  of  the  lower  class  of  people,  in 
the  several  changes  it  has  undergone.  London:  Printed  for  IV. 
Owen.    1748.     8vo,  pp.  71.  j.c.B.  15969 

The  question  is  discussed  of  permitting  foreigners  to  settle  in  the  colonies.  The 
conclusion  arrived  at  by  the  writer  is,  that  it  is  not  advantageous,  and  that  *'  it  is 
more  eligible  to  increase  our  own  people  than  to  invite  over  strangers,  which  is  to  be 
chiefly  done  by  encouraging  matrimony."  The  subject  of  holding  slaves  is  also  dis- 
cussed, and  arguments  for  and  against  it  presented.

The  object  of  the  writer  is  to  show  that  the  colonies  cannot  exist  if  Great  Britain

trade,  the  boasted  power  and  strength  of  the  rebellious  colonies  must  soon  be  annihilated, 
and  they  themselves  be  rendered  sensible  of  their  own  folly,  weakness,  and  total  inability 
to  subsist  for  any  length  of  time,  as  a  nation  divided  from,  or  in  a  state  of  war  with 
Great  Britain." — i'ag^  7.

The  Constitutions  of  the  several  Independent  States  of  Amer- 
ica ;  The  Declaration  of  Independence;  The  Articles  of  Con- 
federation between  the  said  States;  The  Treaties  between  His 
Most  Christian  Majesty  and  The  United  States  of  America. 
Published  by  order  of  Congress.  Philadelphia :  F.  Bailey, 
M.  Dcc.  Lxxxi.     Sm.  8vo,  pp.  226.  C,  H.  16086 

200  copies  printed.  The  first  authoritative  and  original  printed  text  of  these  im- 
portant documents.  "In  Congress,  December  29,  1780.  Resolved,  That  a  Com- 
mittee of  three  be  appointed  to  collect  and  cause  to  be  published  two  hundred  correct 
copies  of  the  Declaration  of  Independence,  etc.  (as  in  title  above),  to  be  bound  to- 
gether in  boards,  etc.  Extract  from  the  Minutes.  Charles  Thompson,  Secretary." 
—  Page  2.  "This  interesting  publication  contains  a  greater  portion  of  unsophisticated 
wisdom  and  good  sense,  than  is  perhaps  to  be  met  with  in  any  other  legislative  code 
that  was  ever  yet  framed.  It  is,  in  short,  the  book  which  may  be  considered  as  the 
Magna  Charta  of  the  United  American  States."  —  M.  Rev.,  Lxvin.  184.  It  is 
scarcely  necessary  to  say  that  this  important  work  has  been  repeatedly  reprinted.  We 
have  given  below  a  chronological  series  of  so  many  as  have  come  under  our  notice. 
The  titles  vary  somewhat,  but  the  substance  is  the  same.

The  Constitutions  of  the  several  Independent  States  of  Amer-

visional Treaty  with  America;  and  (never  before  Published)  an

xxix,  In  Congress,  i  1.,  pp.  472,  List  of  Presidents,  i  1. 

The  portrait  is  devoid  of  any  resemblance  to  the  subject.  "The  American  Con- 
stitutions differ  in  many  particulars,  some  partaking  more,  others  less  of  the  nature 
of  a  pure  democracy;  but  they  are  all  valuable,  because  all  favorable  to  liberty."  — 
Preface.

sions."—  Rich,  i.  239.     Sanguine  for  the  colonies,  but  nothing  new  on  the  subject. 

A  Constitutional  Answer.      See  Wesley  (John). 

Constitutional  Considerations  on  the  Power  of  Parliament  to 
levy  Taxes  on  the  North  American  Colonies.  London.  1766. 
4to.  1 61 3 1 

In  this  rare  pamphlet  the  author  undertakes  to  prove  that  the  colonies  ought  not,  in 
sound  policy,  to  be  allowed  a  representation  in  Parliament,  urging,  as  a  reason,  their 
numbers,  extent,  situation,  and  advantages  of  every  kind,  being  such  that  they  only 
want  a  government  properly  regulated  to  become  the  masters  of  Europe.

We  have  quoted  many  of  these  foreign  editions  of  Cooper,  not  for  their  importance, 
but  because  Europeans  have  imbibed  from  them  much  of  their  "  knowledge  (or  ignor- 
ance) of  American  topics."  For  notices  of  Cooper  and  his  works,  see  "  Am.  M.  Rev.," 
I.  147  ;  II.  411;  "  Am.  Q^  Rev.,"  xvii.  407  j  "Am.  Whig  Rev.,"  iv.  276 ;  "  Ch. 
Exam.,"  XII.  78  ;  "  Dem.  Rev.,' x.  409,513;  xv.  i  ;  xxi.  438;  xxv,  51  ;  "  Eclcc. 
Rev.,"  XI.  31,  410  J  "  Edec.  Mag.,"  ix.  57;  xi.  430;  "  Edin.  Rev.,"  lxxi.  128; 
*•  Fraser's  Mag.,"  xvi.  233;  xix.  371;  "  Knickerb.  Mag.,"  xi.  184;  "  Liv.  Age," 
xxxi.  87;  "Month.  Rev.,"  ex.  122;  *' Museum,"  xv,  510 ;  xvm.  561;  xxxvii.  449; 
"  g.  Rev.,"  Lviii.  286  ;  Lix.  855  "  N.  Am.  Rev,,"  XV.  250 ;  xviii.  314;  xxiii.  150; 
xxvji.  139;  xxxii.  508;  XLVi.  5  ;  xLvii.  448  ;  XLix,  432;  lxxi,  I2i;  "  N.  Y.  Rev.," 
IV.  209;  "South.  Lit,  Mess,,"  IV.  373,724;  v,  169;  vi,  229;  "South,  Rev.,"  v. 
207;  viii.  382;  "  U.  S.  Lit.  Gaz.,"  1.  6,  337;  iv.  87;  vi.  306;  "Westminster 
Rev.,"  XVI.  180.

[Cooper.]  What  think  ye  of  the  Congress  Now.?  or,  An 
Enquiry,  how  far  The  Americans  are  Bound  To  abide  by,  and 
Execute  the  Decisions  of,  the  late  Congress.?  New  Tork:

A  rare  tory  tract  against  the  Congress.  The  following  extract  is  on  the  title-page: 
*'It  is  hoped  and  expected,  that  this  want  of  Confidence  in  the  Justice  and  Tender- 
ness of  the  Mother  Country,  and  this  open  Resistance  to  its  Authority,  can  only  }<ave 
found  place  among  the  lower  and  more  ignorant  of  the  People.  The  better  and  wiser 
part  of  these  Colonies  will  know,  that  Decency  and  submission  may  prevail,  not  only 
to  redress  Grievances,  but  to  obtain  Grace  and  Favour;  while  the  outrage  of  a  public 
violence  can  expect  nothing  but  Severity  and  Chastisement,"  —  General  Conway.  Ap- 
pended to  this  tract,  with  a  separate  title-page  and  paging,  is  the  following  :  *'  What  think 
ye  of  the  Congress  Now?  A  Plan  of  a  Proposed  Union  between  Great- Britain  and  the 
Colonies  of  New  Hampshire,  Massachusetts-Bay,  Rhode  Island,  New-York,  New- 
Jersey,  Pennsylvania,  Maryland,  Delaware,  North  Carolina,  South  Carolina  and  Georgia, 
which  was  produced  by  one  of  the  Delegates  in  Congress  from  Pennsylvania,  as  men- 
tioned in  the  preceding  Work."  \_Ne'w  Tork:  James  Rivington.  1775.]  ^^°>  PP-  4* 
Reprinted  with  the  following  title  : 

[Cooper.]  What  think  ye  of  the  Congress  now,?  Or,  an 
Enquiry  how  far  the  Americans  are  bound  to  abide  by  and  exe- 
cute the  Decisions  of  the  Late  Continental  Congress.  With  a

514  COOPER. 

ington.      London:    reprinted  f.r   Richardson  ^    Urquhart.     1775. 
8vo,  pp.  90.  1^591 

"Intendea  to  dissuade  the  people  of  New  York  from  concurring  with  their  si;rer 
colonies  in  adhering  to  the  association,  etc.,  of  the  Congress."  —  M.  Rev.  Thr  author 
of  this  tract,  quoting  frorr  the  instiuctions  given  to  the  delegates  to  the  Continental 
Congress  by  the  colonies,  undertakes  to  show  that  their  desire  was  to  recover  ii.t;ir  just 
rights  and  liberties,  civil  and  religious,  and  to  adopt  a  plan  for  the  purpose  of  obtaining 
a  redress  of  grievances.  That  the  Congress  transcended  their  powers,  that  the  New 
England  Colonies  and  Pennsylvania  had  been  the  cause  of  the  rebellious  acts  of  the 
Congress,  which  he  charactpriies  as  hideous  and  detestable.  He,  therefore,  calls  upon 
the  people  of  New  York  to  reject  these  acts,  and  withdraw  from  the  association.  "  The 
first  appearance  of  this  Congress,"  the  writer  says,  "raised  our  curiosity,  but  excited  no 
terror.  But  it  was  not  long  before  it  turned  out  to  be  a  perfect  monster  —  a  mad,  blind 
monster!"

—  A  Sermon  Preach'd  April  9.  1760,  at  the  Ordination  Of  the  Reverend  Mr. 
Joseph  Jackson,  To  the  Pastoral  Care  of  the  Church  in  Brooklyn New-Eng- 
land:  John  Draper.  1760.  8vo,  pp.46.  BA.  —  Dr.  Cooper  is  supposed  to  be  the 
autiior  of  "Two  Letters  to  a  Friend  on  the  Present  Critical  Conjuncture  of  Affairs  in 
North  America,  with  an  Account  of  the  Action  at  Lake  George  ...  Boston.  1755." 
pp.  545  also  of  "  Hancock's  Fifth  of  March  Oration."  Dr.  Cooper  graduated  at 
Harvard  College  in  1743,  and  soon  exhibited  such  marks  of  genius,  that,  at  the  age 
of  twenty,  he  was  invited  by  the  church  in  Brattle  Street,  Boston,  to  become  their 
pastor.  His  reputation  increased,  and  he  became  one  of  the  most  popular  preachers 
in  the  country,  and  was  a  contributor  to  many  of  the  political  journals  ofhis  day.

Cooper  (T.)  An  Account  of  the  Trial  of  Thomas  Cooper, 
of  Northumberland ;  on  a  Charge  of  Libel  against  the  President

In  a  catalogue  of  the  sale  of  the  so-called  "Mondidier"  collection  in  London,  the 
following  note  is  appended:  "This  almost  unknown  tract,  published  under  the 
authority  and  at  the  expense  of  the  Archbishop  of  Mexico,  contains  the  date  of  1544, 
both  at  the  beginning  and  end,  and  has  hitherto  been  considered  the  first  book  printed 
in  the  New  World.  A  manuscript  note  of  the  late  owner  says,  *  It  is  unknown  to, 
or  at  least,  unmentioned  by  all  bibliographers  I  have  consulted.  Even  Ternaux  does 
not  seem  to  be  aware  of  its  existence.  This  is  the  only  copy  I  ever  saw  or  heard  of 
in  Mexico  or  elsewhere."*  It  is  scarcely  necessary  to  say  that  the  claim  as  to  priority 
of  printing  is  a  mistake.  Sefior  Icaibalceta,  of  Mexico,  describes  three  books  of  an 
earlier  date,  and  three  others  dated  the  same  year.  Jocher  remarks  :  "  Petrus  de  Cor- 
duba,  ein  spanischer  Dominicaner  von  Corduba,  welches  auch  sein  Geschlechts- 
Nahme  war,  gebohren  um  1460,  studirte  zu  Salamanca  die  Rechte,  trat  aber  her- 
jiach  daselbst  in  obgedachten  Orden,  gieng  1 5 10  als  Missionarius  nach  Domingo, 
wurde  Provincial  seines  Ordens,  schrieb  tl  vocabulario  en  lengua  zapotecoy  und  starb 
den  29  Jun.  1525."

enrichie  de  figures.  A  Paris :  Chez.  Andres  Cailieau.  m  dcc  xxii. 
2  vols.,  8vo,  Title,  pp.  438,  4,  2,  Map  and  Plates  ;  Title,  pp.

Republished  with  the  title  : 

CoREAL.  Recueil  de  Voyages  dans  I'Amerique  Meridionale. 
Amsterdam.    1738.     3  vols.,  i2mo.     Plates.  16782 

For  the  contents  and  collation,  lee  [Bernard),  Vol.  ii.,  No.  4937^,  and  correct  the 
date  to  1738.  The  fact  that  no  Spanish  edition  of  these  voyages  is  known,  has  led  to 
the  belief  that  no  such  person  as  Coreal  ever  lived.  In  speaking  of  the  work,  Mr.  Rich 
says  :  "There  appears  to  be  no  reason  to  doubt  of  the  identity  of  Francis  Coreal,  or  of 
the  fact  of  his  having  visited  the  different  countries  he  describes.  His  relation  occu- 
pies only  one  half  the  work,  the  other  half  being  made  up  of  a  selection  from  other 
authors,  to  which  probably  the  judgment  of  Marchand,  as  quoted  by  Meusel,  applies, 
viz.,  that  the  work  consisted  of  fragments  taken  from  several  effective  voyages  by 
some  starving  compiler.  A  certain  Rev.  Doctor  [Dibdin]  '  stuck  his  spuis  so  li.ird 
into  the  side  of  his  good  bibliographical  courser,'  that  he  leapt  over  a  good  part 
of  the  title  of  this  book,  as  given  in  Meusel,  and  makes  out  Cortal's  '  Voyages 
en  les  Indes  occidentales,'  &c.,  to  be  a  translation  of  the  Flemish  journal  of  Capt. 
Abel  Jansen  Tasman  !  "  This  Flemish  journal  is  merely  one  of  the  before-mentioned 
relations.      Su  Rich,  i.  30-31.

says  the  Spanish  editions  of  Cortes'  letters  are  of  the  greatest  rarity,

Second  edition  of  the  fourth  letter.  Mr.  J.  C.  Brown  has  the  only  copy  known. 
Gayangos,  1866,  p.  vii,  is  certainly  wrong  in  naming  this  as  a  Saragassa  imprint, 
and  the  date  July  8,  1526.  The  three  letters  were  first  collectively  printed  by  Barcia 
in  his  "  Historiadores  primitivos  de  las  Indias  occidentales,  &c."  Vol.  i.  (called  four 
letters  by  Barcia)  separately  paged,  pp.  173.      Madrid.    I'J^f).

Motto.]  I  —  I  Con  las  Licencias  necesarias|  £«  Mexico  en  la  Im^

Plates,  viz.  :  Title,  Engr.  Front.,  2  1.,  Ded.,  6  1.,  Err.,  i  ]., 
Pro!.,  2  1.,  Viage,  pp.  xvi,  Engr.  Map  fold.,  Engr.  View  of 
Temple,  Advertcncias  and  Gobierno,  pp.  36,  Carta,  pp.  37-175,
