Carta,  pp.  329-400,  Indice,  9  1.  j.c.Bt.  16938 

Contains  the  second,  third,  and  fourth  Spanish  letters  of  Cortes,  being  the  second 
collection  of  them  published  to  that  date.  This  collection  led  to  the  appearance  of 
several  translations,  as  will  be  seen  below.  The  notes  by  Loienzana  are  of  value, 
being  chiefly  on  local  questions.  "  Clavigero,  Messico.  i.  25,  says  the  explanations 
of  the  Cordillera  or  tribute  rolls  are  very  inaccurate.  These  rolls,  of  which  Boturini 
had  a  native  painted  copy,  are  also  copied  into  Kingsborough's  work  by  Aglio  from  an 
outline  pen-drawing  of  them  preserved  in  the  Mendoza  collection  in  the  Bodleian 
Library  at  Oxford."  —  Prescott,  i.  40,  note.  Lorenzana,  in  the  "  Prologo,"  says  he 
means  to  reprint  the  second  and  third  letters,  but  he  adds  the  fourth  also.  The 
"Gobierno  Politico,"  pp.  11-36  is  by  Vetancourt,  apparently  from  an  unpublished 
manuscript. 

Cortes.  Historia  de  Mejico,  escrita  por  su  esclarecido  con- 
quistador Hernan  Cortes:  aumentada  con  otros  documentos  y 
notas,  por  D.  Francisco  Antonio  Lorenzana,  Antiguo  Arzobispo 
de  Mejico.  Revisada  y  adaptada  a  la  ortografia  moderna,  por 
D.  Manuel  del  Mar.  Nueva  York:  Sres.  IVhite,  Gallagher  y 
IVhite.  1828.  Large  8vo,  Title,  Av.,  2  1.,  Notic.  Hist.,  etc., 
pp.  5-1 10,  blank,  i  1.,  Prol.,  pp.  vi,  Gobierno,  pp.  7-42,  Cartas, 
pp.  43-614.      Front,  and  3  Plates  Lithog.  ^6939 

Imperfect  reprint  of  Lorenzana.  Icazbalceta's  Colleccion,  i.  xxxv,  notes  the  various 
omissions,  the  addition  of  plates  copied  from  Clavigero,  and  is  out  of  all  patience  at 
the  modernized  spelling.  This  edition  was  gotten  up  for  the  Mexican  market,  with  a 
life  of  Cortes  by  R.  C.  Sands.      Siic  infra.

A  beautiful  piece  of  typography.  In  the  **  Al  Lector,"  on  verso  of  title,  Sen.  Icaz- 
balceta  says,  that  after  printing  a  first  edition  of  sixty  copies,  he  had  reprinted  this 
letter  in  his  Colleccion,  1858,  i.  [pp.  470-483],  and  tliat  he  now  reprints  an  edi- 
tion of  seventy  copies,  of  which  ten  only  are  offered  for  sale.  This  was  a  secret  letter 
to  the  Emperor,  accompanying  Cortes'  fourth  letter.  Gayangos,  1866,  pp.  325-339} 
gives  it,  with  slight  variations,  from  the  Munoz  collection  of  MS.,  adding  that  the  ori- 
ginal is  in  Simancas.     The  date  is  given  as  Oct.  14  in  one  copy. 

Cortes.  Cartas  y  Relaciones  |  de  |  Hernan  Cortes  |  al  Empera- 
dor  Carlos  V|colegidas  e  ilustradasj  por|  Don  Pascual  de  Gayan- 
gos, I  &c  &c  I  Paris  Imprenta  Central  de  los  Ferro-Carriles\  A  Chaix 
y  C^  I  ^c I  1866. 1  Rl.  8vo,  Title,  Introd.,  etc.,  pp.  li,  Text, 
576,  last  blank.  j.C.Bt.  16942 

Gayangos  is  the  author  of  the  **  History  of  the  Mohammedan  Dynasties  of  Spain,'* 
and  the  translator,  with  Vedia,  of  "  Ticknor's  Histoi-y  of  Spanish  Literature."  This 
is  the  only  collection  of  all  the  known  writings  of  Cortes,  and  of  papers  directly  relat- 
ing to  him.  The  collection  consists  of  thirty  pieces,  that  may  be  classified  as  follows  : 
Letters  and  memorials  to  the  Emperor  and  Queen,  i6;  letters  and  memorials  of  an 
official  nature,  5  ;  narratives  relating  to  occurrences  in  Mexico,  5  ;  reports,  orders,  etc., 
4.     A  small  edition  only  was  printed,  of  which  but  few  were  offered  for  sale.

This  must  be  the  fifth  letter,  discovered  shortly  after  Folsom's  translation  of  the 
first  dispatches  had  been  published.

Contains  the  "  De  Insulis"  of  Martyr,  the  second  and  third  letters  of  Cortes,  with 
the  two  dedications,  &c.,  as  in  the  editions  of  1524;  also  missionary  reports,  here  first 
published,  and  reprinted  in  1543,  in  the  "  Thesoro  de  virtudes,"  in  Spanish,  and 
all  again,  in  1555  and  161 6,  in  the  "Novus  Orbis,"  in  Latin.

Contains  second  letter  only,  translated  by  Liburnius  from  the  Latin  of  Savorgnanus  as 
published  the  same  year.  An  Italian  version  of  the  letters  appeared  in  the  "Collection 
of  Ramusius,"  Vol.  in.,  1556,  fols.  225-296  verso,  in  which  the  second,  third,  and 
fourth  letters  are  given,  newly  translated  from  the  Spanish,  the  third  and  fourth  not 
having  been  translated  before.  Ramusius,  fol.  225,  says,  that  after  diligent  research  he 
could  not  discover  the  first  letter.  He  also  gives  the  letter  of  Alvarado  and  Godoy  to 
Cortes,  which  were  appended  to  the  Spanish  edition  of  the  "Cuarta  Carta."  He 
then  adds  the  "  Relatione  di  alcune  cose  della  Nuona  [i/c]  Spagna,  &  della  gran 
citta  di  Temestitan  Messico.  fatta  per  vno  gentil'homo  del  Signor  Ferdinando  Cortese,'* 
fols.  304  verso  to  310,  with  two  cuts.  The  original  Spanish  of  this  is  not  known. 
Icazbalceta,  Coll.  i.  368-398,  copies  Ramusius,  including  the  plates,  and  adds  a 
Spanish  translation  of  this  curious  paper,  which  he  heads  "El  Conquistador  Anonimo." 
This  is  followed  by  the  "  Narrative  of  Alvar  Nunez  Cabeza  de  Vaca,"  fols.  310-330, 
verso.  Next  comes  a  "Relation  of  Nuno  de  Guzman,"  dated  from  Omitlan,  in 
Mechoucan,  July  8th,  1530,  fols.  331-339,  which  is  not  copied  by  Icazbalceta,  nor 
does  it  seem  to  have  been  elsewhere  published.  Another  piece  not  found  in  any  other 
publication,  the  "  Narrative  of  Francisco  Ulloa,"  by  an  officer  of  the  only  vessel  that 
returned   out   of  three  sent  out  by  Cortes,   is  next   given,  fols.   339-354-     Prescott'a 


CORTES.  565 

** Mexico,"  III.  337,  note,  refers  to  this  account.  Ramusius  closes  his  Mexican  docih- 
mcnts  with  reports  by  Vasquez  di  Coronado,  Fray  Marco  d.i  Nizza,  and  Hernando 
Alarcon.  This  tliird  volume  appeared  again  in  1565  and  1606.  See  Ramusius.  The 
"Istoiia  dclla  conquista  del  Mexico,"  Florence^  1660  and  1699,  etc.,  is  from  Dc  Solis.

This  title,  copied  from  a  foreign  bookseller's  list,  seems  to  be  the  second  letter  only, 
and  it  was  not  printed  before  1523,  the  first  Spanish  edition  being  dated  Nov.  8, 
1522.  It  is  not  in  the  *'  Bibl.  Am.  Vet."  of  Harrisse,  nor  docs  it  seem  to  have  been 
noticed  by  any  bibliographer.  The  bookseller  ascribes  to  it  the  date  of  1522  or  1523, 
and  thinks  that  it  is  composed  from  the  second  and  third  letters  of  Cortes. 

A  French  tr.mslation  of  the  second  and  third  letters,  in  an  abridged  form,  appears  at 
the  close  of  the  "  Extrait  ov  recevil  de  Isles  nouuellement  f.ouuces  en  la  grand  mcr 
Oceane,"  etc.,  Paris,  1532,  which  is  said  by  Harrisse,  "Bibl.  Am.  Vet,"  p.  286, 
to  be  a  translation  of  our  No.  16952.  On  leaf  155,  begins  the  "Epitome  de  les 
Seconde  et  Tierce  Narrationes  de  la  mar  Oceane  de  Ferdinant  Cortese,  translatee  dc 
latin  en  francoys."  Another  French  work,  entitled  "Voyages  es  conquetcs  du  capi- 
taine  Ferdinand  Courtois  es  Indes  occidentales,  traduite  de  langue  espagnole  par  Guil- 
laume  le  Bieton,  Nivernois.  Paris.  Abel  VAngelier.  1588,"  8vo,  is  from  Oviedo 
and  Gomara.  (Brunet.)  The  "  Histoire  de  la  Conqueste  du  Mexique  ou  de  la 
nouvelle  Espagne,  par  Fernando  Cortes,"  1691  and  other  dates,  is  a  transhtion  of  De 
Solis.  The  "Aventures  et  conquetes  de  Cortes  au  Mexique,  par  Henri  Lebrun, 
Tours.  1843,"  fourth  edition,  sm.  8vo,  is  also  probably  from  De  Solis.  The  account 
in  the  "Hist.  gen.  des  voyages.  1754,"  Vol.  xii.,  is  from  Herrera,  etc.

additional  notes,  and  an  appendix,  completing  tiie  life  of  Cortes.  The  Gottfried 
collection,  Leyderty  Van  dtr  Aa^  1706  and  1707,  folio  and  xzmo,  has  a  translation 
relating  to  Cortes,  from  Herrera,  entitled,  '*  Roem-Waardige  Zee-  en  Land-togtea 
na  Nieuw-Spanje  en  Mexico."

from  Lorenzann,  including  a  portion  of  his  notes,  ^^f  "  N.  Am.  Rev.,"  lvii.  459. 
The  second  letter,  translated  from  Flavigny,  by  Mr.  Alsop,  appeared  in  the  "  Fort-

the  Conquest  of  the  West  India,"  etc.,  translated  by  T.[homas]  N.[ichols].  London.

"Coll.  de  Doc.  para  la  Hist,  de  Mexico,"  1858-66,  Tom.  i. ;  Prescott's  "  Conq.  of 
Mexico,"  1843  ;  and  numerous  valuable  references  in  Harrisse's  "Bib.  Am.  Vetustiss.,'* 
1866.  It  is  remarkable  that  De  Bry  should  not  have  illustrated  the  "Conquest  of 
Mexico,"  and  that  Grynaeus,  in  his  "  Novus  Orbis"  of  1532,  should  omit  all  notice  of
